coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots derived from ampicillin sodium for live/dead microbe differentiation, bioimaging and high selectivity detection of 2,4-dinitrophenol and Hg(II)
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Xiaotian
Tu, Yujiao
Chen, Wei
Xu, Zhiwei
Wei, Yunlin
Qin, Kunhao
Zhang, Qi
Xiang, Yingying
Zhang, Hongcai
Ji, Xiuling
coredata.dc:description: A highly photoluminescent small-size carbon dots (CDs) deriving ampicillin sodium were successfully obtained via one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CDs showed uniform particle size and possessed good excitation-dependent characteristic. The dead bacteria and yeast cells could be selectively stained by the CDs, but the live ones not. It indicated that the CDs possessed a good potential for the live/dead bacteria and yeast differentiation. Cell and plant leaf imaging showed that the prepared CDs possessed low biotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence emission properties making it a good candidate for bioimaging application. The probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity to detect Hg(II) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), and excellent linear range of 0–50 μM for Hg(II) (R2 = 0.9920) and 0–25 μM for 2,4-DNP (R2 = 0.9919), with low detection limits of 33.40 nM and 13.44 nM for Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP, respectively. It revealed that dynamic quenching mechanism dominated in Hg(II) quenching the CDs fluorescent, while the quenching mechanism of 2,4-DNP detection belonged to the inner filter effect. The sensor was successfully applied to determine the Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP in real water samples. It shows that the fluorescent nanoprobe shows great promising potential for the detection of environmental pollutants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ampicillin sodium
Live/dead microbe differentiation
bioimaging
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
Cytotoxicity evaluation of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs
Possible mechanism of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP detection
Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
Fluorescence quenching test of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM (insert: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum (c) XRD pattern (d) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared CDs. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c), and O1s spectra (d).
CLSM images of live/dead bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus) and one yeast (C. podzolicus) stained with prepared CDs at bright field and fluorescence.
CLSM images of HeLa and U2OS cells with CDs.
Plant leaves imaging with injection of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of metal ions (a) and aromatic compounds to the CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations of Hg(II) (0–200 μM) (a) and 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (b). Relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F
0
–F)/F
0 and Hg(II) (0–200 μM) (c) and 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) (d)concentration. Linear relationship between (F
0
– F)/F
0 and Hg(II) (0–50 μM) (e) and 2,4-DNP (0–25 μM) (f) concentration.

UV–visabsorption spectra of CDs, Hg(II), and CDs + Hg(II)(a), CDs, 2,4-DNP, and CDs+2,4-DNP (b); the FL decay lifetime of CDs and CDs + Hg(II)(c), CDs and CDs+2,4-DNP (d); UV–vis absorption of Hg(II) and fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs (e); UV–vis absorption of 2,4-DNP and fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs (f).
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of 2,4-DNP detection.

row.entry: Palm shell powder and triflic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of quenching mechanism and LOD with the reported methods of Hg(II) detection.
Analytical results of 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) in the water samples by CDs (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dots derived from ampicillin sodium for live/dead microbe differentiation, bioimaging and high selectivity detection of 2,4-dinitrophenol and Hg(II)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly photoluminescent small-size carbon dots (CDs) deriving ampicillin sodium were successfully obtained via one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CDs showed uniform particle size and possessed good excitation-dependent characteristic. The dead bacteria and yeast cells could be selectively stained by the CDs, but the live ones not. It indicated that the CDs possessed a good potential for the live/dead bacteria and yeast differentiation. Cell and plant leaf imaging showed that the prepared CDs possessed low biotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence emission properties making it a good candidate for bioimaging application. The probe exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity to detect Hg(II) and 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), and excellent linear range of 0–50 μM for Hg(II) (R2 = 0.9920) and 0–25 μM for 2,4-DNP (R2 = 0.9919), with low detection limits of 33.40 nM and 13.44 nM for Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP, respectively. It revealed that dynamic quenching mechanism dominated in Hg(II) quenching the CDs fluorescent, while the quenching mechanism of 2,4-DNP detection belonged to the inner filter effect. The sensor was successfully applied to determine the Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP in real water samples. It shows that the fluorescent nanoprobe shows great promising potential for the detection of environmental pollutants.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industry, environmental problems have become more and more serious. Among them, heavy metal and nitrophenols pollution, which threaten the environment and human health, are critical issues [1,2]. Due to toxicity, excellent water solubility, non-biodegradability, transition within different environments and trace accumulation in the food chains [3], Hg(II) has attracted intensive attention. As the intermediates for producing dyes, fireworks and rocket fuels, nitrophenols have been widely used, generating contaminants in wastewaters, rivers and atmosphere. Therefore, nitrophenols have been listed as priority pollutants [4,5]. Most of nitroaromatic compounds, such as 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), belonging to organic carcinogens and mutagens, have high toxicity to live organisms [6,7]. Therefore, the analysis of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP is of important practical significance.

Traditional detection approaches for Hg(II) include atomic absorption spectrometry, plasma mass spectrometry and X-ray fluorescence [2,8,9]. The detection techniques of 2,4-DNP include mass spectrometry, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray imaging and enzyme linked immunosorbent assay [10,11]. Though those methods show good sensitivity and accuracy, they are normally expensive, time-consuming and inconvenient in operation. It is highly necessary to develop a rapid, high-sensitivity, and easily-operable method to monitor the level of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP in different environments.

Since carbon quantum dots (CDs) were first reported in 2004, they have become the famous carbon nanomaterial and attracted more and more attention because of their several advantages, for example high fluorescence intensity, high stability, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [12–15]. They showed good application in detecting metal ions, anions, small organic molecules, bioimaging and drug delivery [16–20].

Ampicillin sodium (C₁₆H₁₈N₃NaO₄S) belongs to broad-spectrum beta-lactam antibiotic that can fight bacteria, whether positive or negative bacteria [21]. Since ampicillin sodium contains C, N, O and S, it can be used for the doping in the CDs without any other additive's addition. In this study, a novel highly photoluminescent small-size CDs deriving ampicillin sodium were successfully obtained via simple one-step hydrothermal method. It was found that 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) could quench the fluorescence of the prepared CDs, and with the concentration increase of 2,4-DNP and Hg(II), the quenching degree increased. To our knowledge, there is the first report on CDs nanoprobe deriving ampicillin sodium for detection of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP.

Fluorescence staining is normally used to evaluate bacterial vitality [22]. As traditional commercial fluorescent dyes, propidium azide and propidium iodide, which can penetrate the cell wall of dead bacteria, are commonly used in DNA dying. However, these commercial dyes are usually expensive, toxic and photobleaching. So, it is urgent to develop dyes with low toxicity, water solubility and low cost to monitor the viability of bacteria. In the paper, the ampicillin sodium-derived fluorescent CDs showed a good potential in live/dead bacteria and yeast differentiation and bioimaging.

Morphology and particle size distribution of CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands) (Fig. 1 a). The CDs showed uniform spherical shapes with 3.2–8.8 nm particle size, and the average diameter was about 4.5 nm. The surface chemistry of the prepared CDs was specify described with fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany) (Fig. 1b). A strong absorption peak at 3,446 cm−1 displays the present of N–H or O–H bonds [23]; the peak at 1,637 cm−1 shows the appears of CC or N–H bonds; the peak at 1,451 cm−1 indicates CC or C–H bonds; the peak at 1,340 cm−1 reveals OC–H or C–N bonds [24]; the absorption peak at 998 cm−1 stands for C–H bond; the peak at 698 cm−1 assigns to O–H or C–H bonds [24]; and the peak at 582 cm−1 indicates C–S stretching vibration. The XRD pattern of the CDs displayed good crystallinity with a broad peak at 2θ = 21°, revealing the CDs with little crystalline nature (Fig. 1c).

The distinct peaks at 285.06 eV, 531.84 eV, 399.91 eV, 1071.3 eV and 163.34 eV are presented by the full scan XPS spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.), attributed to C1s, O1s, N1s, Na1s and S2p, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The atom ratios of the elements are 66.70%, 18.66%, 7.04%, 6.24% and 1.03%. Four peaks from C1s spectrum at 284.3 eV, 284.95 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.2 eV, attributed to CC, C–C or C–N, C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. 2b) [25]. It is fitted into two peaks at 399.7 eV and 400.4 eV from N1s spectrum, attributed to N–H and C–H, respectively (Fig. 2c) [25]. Two peaks from O1s spectrum at 531.2 eV and 532.1 eV, assigned to CO and C–O (Fig. 2d) [25]. XPS diagram results are consistent with those of FTIR spectroscopy. It indicated that there should be abundant hydrophilic and amino-containing functional groups, which confer good water solubility on the prepared CDs [26].

The optical properties of the prepared CDs were performed with UV–vis absorption spectrum (UV-2600 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence emission spectrum (G9800A fluorospectrophotometer, Agilent Technologies, USA) [23]. The liquid of CDs exhibits light yellow under daylight illumination and blue color after UV excitation, indicating that the fluorescent CDs have been successfully prepared (Fig. 1d, insert). It shows a weak absorption peak around 258 nm, ascribing to the π-π* transitions (CC bond), and an obvious absorption peak at 346 nm, assigning to aromatic n-π* transition (CO bond), respectively (Fig. 1d) [23,26–28]. A maximum emission wavelength is 448 nm under the optimal excitation wavelength (346 nm) (Fig. 1d). When the excitation wavelength shifts (300–390 nm), the emission peak shows red-shift, and the emission intensity gradually decreases when the excitation wavelengths above 340 nm, so the prepared CDs exhibit good excitation-dependent feature (Fig. S1), and indicated that they can be used for multicolor cell imaging.

The fluorescence stability of the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs was investigated under different salt concentration and pH. The PL intensity of the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs is substantially constant (Fig. S2a). It is observed that with the NaCl solution concentration increase (0–1.0 mM), the PL intensity only slightly fluctuates without significant change. With the pH buffer changing from 2.2 to 9.0, the fluorescence intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs increases, and then decreases at 9.0–12.0 (Fig. S2b), which demonstrates that prepared ampicillin sodium-derived CDs have optimal pH at 9.0, and have the potential to be used as pH fluorescent nanoprobe.

The quantum yield (QY) of the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs was 12.96% based on standard quinine sulfate (QY = 54%). The fluorescence decay lifetime was determined to be 1.52 ns (τ1 = 1.47 ns, A1 = 20888.08, τ2 = 4.12 ns, A2 = 2,069,940).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent multicolor probes of prepared CDs were designed for bacterial and yeast imaging. But the live bacteria and yeast could not be labeled by the CDs (Fig. 3 ). Then the dead bacteria and yeast were evaluated. Hua et al. treated live Staphylococcus aureus to obtain dead cell with different reagent s for 2 h, including 75% ethanol solution, silver ion, benzalkonium bromide, vancomycin, tetracyclin, and the results showed all the dead S. aureus cell could be stained by CDs-S. aureus [22]. It indicated that the bacterial killing methods had no effect on the live/dead bacterial differentiation with CDs-S. aureus [22]. So, we referred and treated Escherichia coli, S. aureus and Cryptococcus podzolicus only with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h. It revealed that the dead E. coli, S. aureus and C. podzolicus could be distinctly labelled with CDs (50 μg/mL) and observed with blue, green and red colors by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Fig. 3), thus the CDs could be a good candidate for multicolor bioimaging [22].

It indicated that the prepared CDs could successfully discriminate live/dead bacteria and yeast with excellent selectivity. It was deduced that the main determinants of live/dead cell differentiation may be electrostatic interaction between CDs and microbe, and the selective permeability of microbe towards CDs [22]. The zeta average potential was -12.5 mV (Microtrac Inc. Zeta potential analyzer), revealing the presence of negatively charged groups on the prepared CDs surfaces, such as carboxyl groups [29]. The zeta potential values of live E. coli, S. aureus and C. podzolicus measured are -43.63 mV, -30.50 mV and -36.40 mV, respectively. So the prepared CDs cannot label live bacterial cells because of the stronger electrostatic repulsion between CDs and live bacteria. After treatment with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h, the zeta potential values of dead E. coli, S. aureus and C. podzolicus measured are -23.07 mV, -19.03 mV and -22.37 mV, respectively, indicating that electrostatic interaction between CDs surface and dead bacteria is greatly weakened. It is well known that microbial cell membranes have selective permeability, and small molecular substances entering cells usually require transportation by ion channels or transmembrane proteins, so live microbe cells could not be labelled. However, the cell walls or membranes of dead microbe was destroyed, then electrostatic interaction between CDs surface and microbe greatly weakened, and selective permeability deduced, so CDs could successfully label the dead cells. Hua et al. reported the microbial live/dead differentiation with CDs derived from S. aureus and E. coli [22], but there is no report on microbial live/dead differentiation with CDs derived from ampicillin sodium. Therefore, as an effective fluorescent probe, prepared ampicillin sodium-derived CDs with high PL intensity showed excellent application prospects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity evaluation of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study the bioimaging application potential of the prepared ampicillin sodium-derived CDs, the cytotoxicity was investigated with 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di-phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) assays. The viability of the HeLa and U2OS cells cultivated with ampicillin sodium-derived CDs was measured, and at the same time, untreated cells were taken as control. Cell viability of HeLa cell showed a decrease when CDs concentration higher 8.5 μg/mL (Fig. S3), while the cell viability of HeLa cell and U2OS cell were over 50% even the CDs concentration at 17,000 μg/mL. Therefore, the prepared CDs showed low cytotoxicity to HeLa and U2OS cell.

Biocompatibility is one of the important determinants of nanomaterials appilication in medical diagnosis. In this study, the good biocompatibility and mulcolor PL indicated that the prepared CDs were suitable for bioimaging applications, therefore, in vivo cell imaging was performed by CLSM. The HeLa and U2OS cells emitted different colors of fluorescence in the cytoplasm after incubation at 50 μg/mL ampicillin sodium-derived CDs for 2 h, and were clearly stained by CDs. It could be clearly observed in all three channels of blue, green and red when at 405, 488 and 543 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, which suggested that the CDs could be effectively internalized (Fig. 4 ). The CLSM results clearly demonstrated the potential of the CDs for optical bioimaging at the cellular level. In addition, no substantial morphological changes were observed in Hela and U2OS cells treated with the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs, which further confirmed the low cytotoxicity of the CDs.

The plant imaging was performed to verify the biodistribution of CDs in plant leaf. The fluorescence intensity increased over time (Fig. 5 ), indicating that CDs successfully entered the leaves system of Amaranthus viridis L. and Nicandra physaloides, and the fluorescence generation sites were spread throughout the leaves cells rather than attached to the injection sites of leaves. Thus, the CDs can be distributed throughout the leaves body via leaf vein. It indicated that the prepared CDs functioned with excellent biocompatibility and were rapidly transported in the leaves. The plant leaf bioimaging successfully broadens the application of CDs in bioimaging, and provides a new perspective for plant marker research.

The selectivity of the ampicillin sodium-derived CDs was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of different possible interfering substances was monitored, such as P, NP, 4-CP, PAP, 2,4-DCP, 4-NP, MAP, 2,4,6-TCP, 2,4-DNP, OAP, Pb(II), Fe(III), Cu(II), Cr(III), Hg(II), Cd(II), Ni(II), Mn(II), Co(II), Be(II), Mg (II), Al(III), Ag(l) and Cs(I). Only 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) could effectively quench the PL intensity of the prepared CDs (Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b). Therefore, high selectivity exists between Hg(II)/2,4-DNP and ampicillin sodium-derived CDs. This demonstrates that ampicillin sodium-derived CDs can be used to detect Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP.

The pH affects not only the PL intensity of CDs, but also the quenching efficiency of targets on CDs. The fluorescence intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs is related to pH (Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b). In the pH 2.2–8.0 of Hg(II) detection, the PL intensity increases and obtains maximum quenching efficiency, but after pH 9.0, the PL intensity decreases. It shows that the fluorescence quenching reaction reaches the largest at pH 8.0, so pH 8.0 is the optimum pH value of Hg(II) detection. In the pH 2.2–4.0 of 2,4-DNP detection, the PL intensity increases and reaches the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency, but after pH 4.0, the PL intensity decreases. The results show that the fluorescence quenching reaction reaches the largest at pH 4.0, so the optimum pH value of 2,4-DNP detection is pH 4.0. The effect of pH on CDs can be attributed to the surface charges changes caused by deprotic processes, so it suggests that the CDs could be used in both acidic and weakly alkaline environments.

To investigate the stability of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs in detecting 2,4-DNP and Hg(II), the fluorescence intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs with 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) at 15 μM was performed under different reaction time. The PL intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs with Hg(II) is stable after 2 min (Fig. S4c). Therefore, 2 min was chosen as the optimal fluorescence quenching reaction time of Hg(II) [23]. The PL intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs with 2,4-DNP fluctuates within 0–8 min, then tends to be stable (Fig. S4d). Therefore, 8 min was considered as the optimal fluorescence quenching reaction time of 2,4-DNP. In order to investigate the interfere with each other of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP, the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F
0
–F)/F
0 of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP towards CDs were performed at pH 4.0 and pH 8.0. It showed that the two types of test objects did not interfere with each other at different pHs (Fig. S5).

The relation between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs and Hg(II) or 2,4-DNP concentration was studied under the optimal experimental conditions, and the fluorescence quenching quantitative analysis method was established. As shown in Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b, the PL intensity of ampicillin sodium-derived CDs decreases with the addition of Hg(II)/2,4-DNP concentrations. Relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency (F
0
–F)/F
0 and Hg(II) (0–200 μM) or 2,4-DNP (0–100 μM) concentration shown in Fig. 7c and Fig. 7d. The ratio of PL intensity is linear with the Hg(II) concentration (0–50 μM, R2 = 0.9920) (Fig. 7e). The ratio of PL intensity is linear with the 2,4-DNP concentration (0–25 μM, R2 = 0.9919) (Fig. 7f). The detection limit of 2,4-DNP is 13.44 nM and the S/N is 3 (Table 1 ). The detection limit of Hg(II) is 33.40 nM and the signal-noise ratio is 3(S/N) (Table 2 ). Currenttly, the research on 2,4-DNP focuses on the adsorption and removal of 2,4-DNP in environmental contaminated samples [30,31]. There are relatively few reports on the detection of 2,4-DNP by CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The traditional quenching mechanisms of targets towards fluorescence CDs mainly include 5 types: static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [43,44]. Usually, when the UV absorption spectrum of the quencher overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the CDs, IFE works [43,45]. It leads to an apparent decrease of intensity, and actually it is not a quenching process but attributes to the excitation beam attenuation or reabsorption of emitted radiation by the quencher or excessive CDs [34].

To verify the quenching mechanisms of 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) towards CDs, UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, 2,4-DNP, Hg(II), and CDs with 2,4-DNP or Hg(II) were determined (Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8b). We can find that the absorption of the CDs does not change with or without Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP, which indicates that no new substance has formed. So, they do not belong to the SQ process.

As shown in Fig. 8c and Fig. 8d, the FL decay lifetime decreased without (1.52 ns) or with Hg(II) (0.94 ns), while the FL decay lifetime does not change without (1.52 ns) or with 2,4-DNP (1.49 ns). So the quenching mechanism of Hg(II) may be DQ, FRET or PET, while that of 2,4-DNP should be inner IFE [46].

The absorption spectrum of Hg(II) does not overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the prepared CDs (Fig. 8e), therefore, the mechanism of FRET or IFE was eliminated [47]. The absorption spectrum of 2,4-DNP overlaps with the excitation spectrum of CDs (Fig. 8f). Thus, we inferred maybe IFE dominated in quenching process.

Furthermore, the Parker equation was employed to verify the IFE mechanism [44]:
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
203 d
A
e x
1 −
10
d
A
e x
10 g
A
c o r
203 s
A
e m
1 −
10
s
A
c o r
F
cor and F
obsd are the observed PL intensity and the corrected PL intensity, respectively; A ex and A em are the absorbance value at the maximum excitation wavelength, and the absorbance value at the maximum emission wavelength, respectively; d is the cuvette width; s refers to the excitation beam thickness; g assigns to the distance between the edge of cuvette and excitation beam [44]. With 2,4-DNP concentration increasing (0–25 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value increased from 1.71 to 2.67, which met the Parker equation [39]. Therefore, IFE was considered to be dominated in quenching process of 2,4-DNP towards the fluorescent of CDs. With Hg(II) concentration increasing (0–50 μM), F
cor
/F
obsd value constantly fluctuating, which did not fit the Parker equation. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of Hg(II) did not belong to IFE.

In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation was employed to further investigate the kinetics of dynamic quenching mechanism [44]:
F
0
F
= 1 +
k
q
τ
0
[
Q
]
F
0 is the PL intensity of the prepared CDs, and F is the PL intensity with Hg(II), Kq is the quencher rate coefficient, [Q] is the Hg(II) concentration, τ
0 is the FL decay lifetime of the prepared CDs [44]. A rise of temperature (288.25 K, 298.25 K, 308.25 K) lead to the increase of slope (0.01783, 0.01879, 0.02117) and Kq (1.18 × 1013 M−1‧S−1, 1.23 × 1013 M−1‧S−1, 1.39 × 1013 M−1‧S−1). Combining the effects of UV–vis absorption, temperature effect on Kq and fluorescence lifetime (τ / τ
0 ≠1), the quenching mechanism of Hg(II) for the prepared CDs is further verified to be DQ. Extensive studies on fluorescence sensing of have been reports, and the quenching mechanisms of Hg(II) on CDs fluorescence were usually SQ or PET, while the research on DQ is relatively rare.

The CDs based fluorescent nanoprobe was employed to detect the 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) in real water samples (tap water, river water and waste water). The pHs of tap water, river water and waste water samples were 6.8, 7.2 and 5.0, respectively. The recovery experiments were carried out in a standard addition method. The good percentage recoveries and relative standard deviation (%RSD) were obtained by the analysis. For water samples, the recoveries of 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) were estimated to fall within 95.90–97.50% and 92.30–98.70%, respectively (Table 3 ). It represents the sensibility of CDs on 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) in tap water, river water and waste water (n = 3). Therefore, it is easy, sensitive and fast to detect 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) in real water.

Ampicillin sodium was purchased from Solarbio (Lot No. 128F033); all analytical reagents, including phenol (P), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), nonylphenol (NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP), m-aminophenol (MAP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), p-aminophenol (PAP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), o-aminophenol (OAP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), HgCl2, CuSO4, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, CrCl3, MnSO4, CoCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, BaCl2, Ni(NO3)2, AgCl, CsCl and FeCl3 were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid media, including 10 g/L peptone, 5 g/L yeast extract, and 10 g/L NaCl (pH 7.0), was used to incubate E. coli and S. aureus. YPD liquid media, including 20 g/L peptone, 10 g/L yeast extract, and 20 g/L glucose (pH 5.4), was used to incubate C. podzolicus. Peptone and yeast extract were purchased from Oxoid, Hampshire, UK.

The CDs of ampicillin sodium were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method with ampicillin sodium as material. The 0.6 g ampicillin sodium powder was dissolved in 30 mL sterile water, then transferred to 50 mL high-pressure autoclave, 200 °C reaction for 6 h. The production was cooled naturally, centrifuged (13,000 g, 30 min), filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrane and stored (4 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of live/dead bacteria and yeast
ce:section.ce:para: Two kind of bacteria, E. coli and S. aureus, belonging to Gram-negative bacterium and Gram-positive bacterium, respectively, and one type of yeast cell (C. podzolicus) were used to study the live/dead microbial differentiation with prepared CDs. E. coli and S. aureus were incubated in LB liquid media (37 °C, 12 h), while C. podzolicus was incubated in YPD media (28 °C, 12 h). They were treated with 2% formaldehyde solution for 2 h to obtain dead cells, at the same time, the live cells treating with phosphate buffer solution as control [22]. All the cells were imaged under a CLSM with excitation at 405, 488 and 543 nm.

HeLa cell and human osteosarcoma cell (U2OS) cell were cultured with 10% fetal bovine serum DMEM medium (5% CO2, 37 °C). Sterilized cell slide was put into a 24-well plate, and then HeLa and U2OS cell were cultured (5% CO2, 37 °C, 24 h) [23,26]. Then, the cell cytotoxicity of the prepared CDs was assessed with MTT method.

After 24 h cultivation, serum starved for 1 h, and removed the medium, then washed the recombinant cell with phosphate buffer solution. Different concentration ampicillin sodium-derived CDs were added and incubated (37 °C, 2 h). All samples were fixed with paraformaldehyde (4%, 30 min) and the cell imaging was performed on CLSM.

To further investigate the biodistribution imaging of the prepared CDs in plant, leaves of Amaranthus viridis L. and Nicandra physaloides, with around 3–5 cm of length, were collected and their surfaces were sterilized. The sterilized leaves were injected with CDs, 100 μg/mL for Amaranthus viridis L. and 50 μg/mL for Nicandra physaloides, then observed under the IVIS Lumina Series III imaging system (PerkinElmer) (exposure time 0.2 s) at regular intervals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching test of Hg(II) and 2,4-DNP
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 2 μL of prepared ampicillin sodium-derived CDs solution and 0.98 mL phosphate buffer solution mixed, different concentration Hg(II)/2,4-DNP solution was added, and then diluted to 4.0 mL with deionized water [23]. The PL intensity was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer after 2 min or 8 min at the slit width of 2.5 nm. The selectivity of Hg(II)/2,4-DNP and other substances was investigated to evaluate the coexisting interfering substances on the PL intensity. Substances include P, 4-CP, NP, PAP, 4-NP, 2,4-DCP, OAP, 2,4-DNP, MAP, 2,4,6-TCP, HgCl2, CuSO4, Pb(NO3)2, AgCl, CdCl2, CrCl3, MnSO4, CoCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, BaCl2, Ni(NO3)2 and CsCl.

The quantum yield (QY) was determined according to the classic operating procedures. The QY of quinine sulfate dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M) was 54% at excited wavelength 360 nm, and assigned as a reference [29]. The QY follows the equation:
Q
CDs = Q
R (I
CDs /I
R ) (A
R /A
CDs ) (η
CDs
2/η
R
2)
Where the subscript CDs refers to prepared CDs and R refers to reference; Q
CDs refers to QY of the prepared fluorescence CDs, I is the integrated emission intensity, η is the solvent refractive index (η
R = 1.33), and A refers to the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength.

Average lifetime model for total lifetime calculation of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is as follows:
τ =
∑
A
n
τ
n
2
A
n
τ
n
( n = 1 , 2 , 3 … )

The fitting model of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is E-exponential model:
Y= B + A1*exp(-i/τ1) + … + An*exp(-i/τn) (n = 1,2,3 ……)

To investigate the practicality of the prepared fluorescence CDs sensor system, the same experiment was performed in tap, lake and waste water with and without 2,4-DNP and Hg(II). The water samples were collected and centrifuged (13,000 g, 15 min) and then filtered by 0.22 μm filter membrance to remove impurities.

A fluorescent CDs derived from ampicillin sodium were prepared by one-step hydrothermal synthesis method. Morphology, chemical element composition and optical properties of the prepared ampicillin sodium-derived CDs were characterized. It possessed excellent fluorescence features. The prepared CDs could selectively differentiate live/dead microbial cells (bacteria and yeast). It showed that the prepared CDs possessed low biotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence emission properties by cell and plant leaf bioimaging, making it a good candidate for bioimaging application. Furthermore, 2,4-DNP and Hg(II) could quench the PL intensity of the prepared CDs. A favourable linear relationship was obtained between the fluorescence reaction of prepared CDs and Hg(II) or 2,4-DNP concentration in the range 0–50 μM and 0–25 μM, with the detection limit 33.40 nM and 13.44 nM under optimized conditions, respectively. It revealed that dynamic quenching mechanism dominated in Hg(II) quenching the CDs fluorescent, while the quenching mechanism of 2,4-DNP detection belonged to the inner filter effect. Thus, as the synthetic method is simple and inexpensive, it is an excellent candidate for bioimaging application and high sensitivity detection of 2,4-DNP and Hg(II).

Xiaotian Yuan: Methodology. Yujiao Tu: Methodology. Wei Chen: Methodology. Zhiwei Xu: Data curation. Yunlin Wei: Writing - review & editing. Kunhao Qin: Investigation. Qi Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Yingying Xiang: Data curation, Validation. Hongcai Zhang: Data curation. Xiuling Ji: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2020.108187.
This work was financially funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (31700324 and 31860147) and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M643535).
